visits.text: Amarbayasgalant Khiid Monastery - 'monastery of tranquil felicity'. One of Mongolia's three largest monasteries, built between 1727 and 1736 on order of Manchu Emperor Yongzheng in memory of Zanabazar, Mongolia's first Buddhist spiritual leader (Bogd Gegen). Construction cost 3,730 kg of silver from imperial state coffers. The architecture is remarkably stylistically coherent: Chinese style of the Manchu period with Mongolian and Tibetan influences in colors and sculptures. The plan is strictly symmetrical on a north-south axis - 28 buildings survive today of the original 40. The Soviets partially destroyed the site in 1937; restoration began in 1988. It's one of the only monasteries to have partially survived the purges, making it the best-preserved in the country.

Iven Valley and foot of Mount Buren-Khaan - the monastery sits in an isolated valley at the foot of a massif of wooded hills. You arrive after 40 km of track through an increasingly narrow and silent valley - the surprise effect on arrival is total. Ovoos crown the surrounding hills.

activities.text: Morning Buddhist ceremony - arrive early (around 8-9am) to attend the monks' morning prayer in the main temple. The drums, brass trumpets and lamas' voices create a particularly striking atmosphere in this remote setting.

Hike to the stupas on the heights - behind the monastery, a trail climbs toward the hills where several white stupas are placed. In 20 to 30 minutes of climbing, the plunging view over the monastery complex and valley justifies the effort.

days_plan.itin_title: Mongolia Road Trip: Gobi Desert, Orkhon Valley & Lake Khövsgöl

places.name: Baga Gazriin Chuluu

visits.text: Baga Gazriin Chuluu Canyon - 'stone of the small place' in Mongolian. This granite massif rises from the flat steppe at 1,751 meters altitude in Dundgovi Province, about 270 km south of Ulaanbaatar. Erosion has carved narrow passages between blocks of granite 210 million years old. Some corridors are barely wide enough to pass through face-forward. Inside the site, you'll find ruins of a 17th-century Buddhist temple destroyed by the Communists in the 1930s, along with inscriptions left in the rock by two hermit monks in the 19th century.

Sacred spring of the rock - a narrow, deep fissure in the granite collects rainwater. Local legend credits it with the power to cure eye diseases; a spoon placed on the edge allows visitors to collect a few drops. Whether you believe it or not, the place is strange and the surrounding scenery is worth the detour.

Hill ovoos - the hills surrounding the canyon are crowned with ovoos, those pyramids of stones and wood to which Mongolians tie khadaks (blue scarves) and circle clockwise to honor the spirits of the place. Clear views across the steppe from the heights.

activities.text: Hike through the granite maze - one to two hours walking through gorges and between granite blocks. The path is unmarked, which gives the route an exploratory feel. Bring sturdy shoes; some passages require squeezing through or light scrambling.
Night sky observation from camp - at this distance from Ulaanbaatar, with no light pollution, the steppe sky is exceptionally clear. Night in ger camp or tent.

days_plan.itin_title: Mongolia Road Trip: Gobi Desert, Orkhon Valley & Lake Khövsgöl
Grand Mongolia Tour: Altai, Gobi, and Central Steppe

map_keywords: Baga Gazriin Chuluu canyon granite Mongolia

visits.text: Bayanzag, the Flaming Cliffs - Roy Chapman Andrews named this site 'the flaming cliffs' during his 1920s expeditions: at sunset, the red sandstone cliffs turn blood red, then burnt orange. Bayanzag means 'rich in saxaul', that twisted, dense shrub that camels browse in the Gobi. In 1923, a member of Andrews' expedition discovered here the first dinosaur eggs ever scientifically identified - Protoceratops eggs. Since then, the site has yielded Velociraptors, Oviraptors and numerous primitive mammal fossils. Fossil collecting is strictly forbidden, but you can see them on site from the ridge trails.

activities.text: Walk along the cliff ridge - a trail runs along the plateau edge about ten meters above the cliffs. Looking down, you sometimes spot bone fragments or suspicious shapes in the rock. The rule: observe, don't take anything. The late afternoon light literally transforms the cliffs into embers.

Saxaul forest at the foot of the cliffs - descend to explore this dwarf forest of shrubs whose wood is so dense it sinks in water. These saxaul have existed since the age of dinosaurs and play a key role in stabilizing Gobi soil. The site is bordered by numerous semi-wild camels.

days_plan.itin_title: Mongolia Road Trip: Gobi Desert, Orkhon Valley & Lake Khövsgöl
Grand Mongolia Tour: Altai, Gobi, and Central Steppe

places.name: Dalanzadgad

visits.text: Yolyn Am Canyon - 'valley of the bearded vulture', in Gobi Gurvansaikhan National Park, 46 km from Dalanzadgad. At 2,500 meters altitude, this narrow gorge cuts through the Gurvan Saikhan mountains for several kilometers. Its walls are so high and so close together that sunlight barely penetrates - result: a field of ice several meters thick forms each winter and melts only in midsummer, if at all some years. In June, you can walk on ice in the middle of the Gobi Desert. The canyon is also home to argali (large wild sheep), ibex and - more rarely - the snow leopard.

Gobi Wildlife Museum at the park entrance - just before the canyon trail, a small museum presents Gobi fauna with taxidermied specimens, eggs and dinosaur bones. Quick but useful for understanding what you might observe in the park.

activities.text: Hike through Yolyn Am gorge - the trail follows a stream and winds between rock walls that gradually narrow until only two people can pass side by side. About 7 km round trip from the parking area, flat with no technical difficulty. Early in the season, much of the canyon floor remains frozen.

Arrival in Dalanzadgad late afternoon - this small town is the administrative capital of Ömnögovi Province (South Gobi) and the main gateway to the Gobi. Take advantage of the local market to stock up before the following stages.

days_plan.itin_title: Mongolia Road Trip: Gobi Desert, Orkhon Valley & Lake Khövsgöl
Grand Mongolia Tour: Altai, Gobi, and Central Steppe

map_keywords: Yolyn Am Canyon Gobi Gurvansaikhan National Park

visits.text: Erdenet - Mongolia's second city (about 90,000 inhabitants) rose from nowhere in 1973: a joint Soviet-Mongolian expedition had finalized plans in 1972 to exploit a massive copper deposit discovered in the 1950s. The mine is now the world's fourth-largest copper mine: 22 million tons of ore are extracted annually, yielding 126,700 tons of copper. In the mid-1980s, over half the inhabitants were Russians; after 1990, most returned home. Their influence is still felt in the architecture: Soviet concrete apartment blocks, wide boulevards. The city is surrounded by green hills and has a functional but pleasant atmosphere, very different from the rest of the circuit.
Soviet-Mongolian Friendship Monument - statue commemorating the alliance between the two countries, typical of the era's iconography. Worth seeing to understand Erdenet's recent history.

activities.text: View of the open-pit mine - at the mining site entrance, accessible by showing your passport, you can observe the Erdenet mine's open pit from the perimeter. A pit several hundred meters deep carved into a hill - an industrial spectacle on a rarely seen scale. (closed Fridays)
Central market and restocking - Erdenet is the last major commercial center before returning to Ulaanbaatar. Well-stocked supermarkets, markets, local restaurants.

days_plan.itin_title: Mongolia Road Trip: Gobi Desert, Orkhon Valley & Lake Khövsgöl

visits.text: Hyargas Nuur Lake - saltwater lake in Uvs province, designated a nature reserve since 2000 and integrated into the Great Lakes Basin UNESCO World Heritage site since 2003. At 75 km long and 31 km wide with an average depth of 50 meters, it's one of the country's four largest lakes. Its semi-desert shores, white marble cliffs to the south, and the emerging Khet-suu Khad rock - serving as a perch for migratory cormorants from April to October - make it a remarkable ornithological site. The lake's south side is particularly photogenic.

activities.text: Migratory bird watching on the lake shores - Hyargas Nuur is a major stopover on the migration route between Siberia and South Asia. Herons, cranes, geese, spoonbills, and, if you're lucky, the white-headed duck (a rare species) are visible in wetland areas bordering the lake. Bring binoculars and visit the shores early morning for best observations.

Horseback or walking excursion around the lake - local providers offer horseback rides along the saline shores. It's an opportunity to cross little-frequented semi-desert steppe zones, observe local wildlife (ibex on heights, marmots), and converse with nomadic families camping near the lake in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Mongolia Tour: Altai, Gobi, and Central Steppe

visits.text: Karakorum / Kharkhorin - Ögödei Khan, son of Genghis Khan, officially founded this capital in 1235. For about forty years, the city was the political, commercial and diplomatic heart of the Mongol Empire, a crossroads on the Silk Road where Venetian merchants, French emissaries and Buddhist monks circulated. In 1260, Kublai Khan transferred the capital to Khanbalik (present-day Beijing); Karakorum declined and was then razed in 1388 by Ming Dynasty troops. Today only four stone tortoises that delimited the city gates remain, along with ongoing archaeological excavations.

Erdene Zuu Monastery - built in 1585 by Abtai Khan directly on the ruins of Karakorum, whose stones it reused. It is the oldest permanent-structure Buddhist monastery in Mongolia. Its white enclosure of 108 stupas (108 is the sacred number in Buddhism, the number of beads on a rosary) measures about 400 meters per side. In 1872, it had 62 temples; in 1939, Communist purges reduced it to three temples and the enclosure wall. These three temples are now active museums, richly decorated with 17th-century thangkas. Listed as a UNESCO World Heritage site in 2004 under 'Orkhon Valley Cultural Landscape'.

activities.text: Visit to the Karakorum Museum - this modern museum displays objects from archaeological excavations of the ancient capital: Chinese pottery, Islamic coins, architectural fragments. Helps understand the cosmopolitan dimension of the Mongol Empire.

Walk around the stone tortoises - the four granite tortoises that supported the steles marking the gates of the ancient capital are scattered at the four corners of the site. Find them in the steppe around Erdene Zuu - two of them are visible from the monastery.

days_plan.itin_title: Mongolia Road Trip: Gobi Desert, Orkhon Valley & Lake Khövsgöl
Grand Mongolia Tour: Altai, Gobi, and Central Steppe

visits.text: Lake Khövsgöl - 'the blue pearl of Mongolia'. 136 km long, 36 km wide, 267 meters deep: Khövsgöl alone contains 2% of the world's freshwater reserves. It was formed by the same tectonic forces as Lake Baikal, located 195 km to the north - the two lakes are connected by a network of rivers. Its waters, exceptionally transparent, can be drunk directly. The southern shore, from Khatgal, looks out onto the pine-covered hills surrounding the lake and the first peaks over 2,000 meters. The lake freezes from January to April.

Village of Khatgal - founded in 1727 as a Mongolian guard post, this village at the lake's southern tip was long a trade center between Mongolia and Russia. It's now the main entry point for travelers. Small wooden town with colorful houses, by the lake.

Lake's eastern shore from Khatgal - the eastern shore is less frequented than the western shore. After a few kilometers' walk beyond the village, you find yourself alone facing the lake, with mountains outlined on the opposite shore. More rolling and wilder than the western shore.

activities.text: Walk along the lake's western shore - from Khatgal, a track runs along the western shore through pine forests and grasslands. Even on foot or by bike, the first kilometers from the village offer direct views onto the lake surface and the Khoridol Saridag mountains opposite.

Kayak or canoe on Lake Khövsgöl - operators in Khatgal rent kayaks to explore the first kilometers of lake. The water is cold even in summer but remarkably clear. Half a day is enough to reach a small isolated cove and enjoy the silence.

Hike in the wooded hills of Khatgal - trails climb into pine forests above the village and provide plunging views over the lake and the entire southern shore. In summer, Tsaatan families (reindeer herders from the taiga) sometimes descend to the Khatgal area - an opportunity for an encounter without the multi-day trek needed to reach their camps in the north. The Tsaatan number fewer than 300 people total, spread across about a dozen families in the northern Khövsgöl taiga.

days_plan.itin_title: Mongolia Road Trip: Gobi Desert, Orkhon Valley & Lake Khövsgöl
Mongolia Road Trip: Gobi Desert, Orkhon Valley & Lake Khövsgöl

visits.text: Khogno Khan granite massif - nicknamed the 'mini-Gobi,' this natural park combines semi-desert steppes, granite rock formations, pine forests, and a long band of sand dunes (Elsen Tasarkhai) stretching 80 km. On the mountainside, Erdene Khamba Monastery (17th century) built by Zanabazar in honor of his spiritual master overlooks the rocky defile. Ruins of Ovgon Khiid Monastery, destroyed in 1939, are found lower in the canyon.

Elsen Tasarkhai sand dunes - these semi-desert dunes, part of the Khugnu Khan mountain chain, constitute a geological curiosity 280 km from Ulaanbaatar. They offer a foretaste of the Gobi, and a few Bactrian camels often graze at the dunes' foot.

activities.text: Horseback ride to Erdene Khamba Monastery - regional providers offer horseback rides to reach the monastery via granite massif tracks. The approach from the heights reveals beautiful views over Elsen Tasarkhai dunes below.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Mongolia Tour: Altai, Gobi, and Central Steppe

places.name: Khongoryn Els

visits.text: Khongoryn Els Dunes - 'Duut Mankhan', the singing dunes, is what Mongolians call them: when wind shifts the sand or you run down from a ridge, the grains emit a deep, hollow sound, a sort of low vibration that can be heard several hundred meters away. These dunes stretch about 180 km long and up to 27 km wide, with peaks reaching 300 meters - three times the height of the Dune du Pilat. They constitute the largest sand area in Mongolia, within Gobi Gurvansaikhan National Park. The pale yellow sand changes hue throughout the day: orange at dawn, blinding white at noon, powder pink at sunset.

Khongoryn Gol Oasis - at the foot of the dunes, on the north side, the small Khongoryn Gol River runs alongside the massif for several kilometers, fed by underground springs. This green strip in an entirely sand landscape is surreal. Nomadic camel-herding families set up their gers here each summer; horses and Bactrian camels graze in the grass just steps from the first dunes.

Panorama from the main ridge - if you couldn't do it the day before, climb at sunrise. The raking light reveals all the undulations of the massif. On clear days, the Gurvan Saikhan mountains close the horizon to the north.

activities.text: Climbing a dune ridge at sunset - the hike up the ridge of a large dune takes 45 to 90 minutes depending on the height chosen. The sand you sink into with each step makes progress exhausting, but the view from the ridge - over the sea of sand on one side, the steppe and mountains on the other - is worth the effort. Bring shoes you can remove; the climb is often done barefoot. Set out at least 2 hours before sunset.

Camelback ride at the foot of the dunes - local operators offer Bactrian camel rides along the oasis edge and into the first dunes. One to two hours to see the landscape from camel height and observe the life of nomadic camel-herding families.

Exploring the oasis and the corridor between dunes and river - follow the bank of Khongoryn Gol eastward or westward, along the boundary between sand and vegetation. The place is particularly photogenic early in the morning. You'll often cross herds of camels and horses coming to drink.
Rest day and recovery - the free day is welcome after several days of intense off-road driving. Free time to read, rest under a ger, chat with nomadic families settled near the camp.

days_plan.itin_title: Mongolia Road Trip: Gobi Desert, Orkhon Valley & Lake Khövsgöl
Mongolia Road Trip: Gobi Desert, Orkhon Valley & Lake Khövsgöl
Grand Mongolia Tour: Altai, Gobi, and Central Steppe
Grand Mongolia Tour: Altai, Gobi, and Central Steppe

map_keywords: Khongoryn Els Singing Sand Dunes Mongolia

visits.text: Khar-Us Nuur Lake - about forty kilometers east of Khovd, this 1,153 km² freshwater lake (the country's second largest) has been a Ramsar site since 1999 for its important wetlands. Its average depth is only 2 to 4 meters, making it a privileged habitat for over 268 bird species. In summer, you can observe pelicans, great egrets, cormorants, and wild ducks. Ak-Bashi Island rises in its center, dividing the lake into two sections. Take time to walk along the marshy shores in search of birds.

Khovd city - founded in 1731 by the Qing dynasty, Khovd is one of Mongolia's oldest cities. Capital of a particularly diverse province (twelve different ethnic groups coexist here), it maintains an authentic atmosphere. Stroll through the market to discover local crafts and specialties.

activities.text: Scenic route Ölgii → Khovd through the Altai foothills - this driving day (about 3.5 hours) introduces you to Mongolian Altai landscapes: dry steppes, river valleys, herds of yaks and horses. Plan photo stops at mountain passes to enjoy panoramas over snow-capped massifs.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Mongolia Tour: Altai, Gobi, and Central Steppe

visits.text: Steppe landscapes between Terkhiin Tsagaan Nuur and Mörön - primarily a transfer day, 360 to 400 km across the Arkhangai and Khövsgöl steppe. The landscapes of rolling grasslands, small rivers and herds of horses roaming free have their own beauty. Plan an early morning departure.

activities.text: Photo stops in the steppe - take advantage of breaks to get out of the vehicle, walk in the steppe and observe raptors (buzzards, falcons) soaring above the grasslands. The Arkhangai sky is often clear and the clouds are spectacular.

Mörön market in the evening - Mörön is the capital of Khövsgöl Province. The local market allows restocking on fresh products before the lake stage: fruits, vegetables, fresh milk, artisanal cheese. Last substantial resupply before Khatgal.

days_plan.itin_title: Mongolia Road Trip: Gobi Desert, Orkhon Valley & Lake Khövsgöl

visits.text: Arrival in Ölgii after approximately 2.5 hours' flight from Ulaanbaatar. Capital of Bayan-Ölgii province, this city of 29,000 inhabitants sits at 1,710 meters altitude and represents a world apart within Mongolia: here, residents speak Kazakh, mosques punctuate the urban landscape (four in total), and colorful embroideries adorn storefronts. Spend the afternoon visiting the provincial museum, which presents local wildlife, history, and Kazakh culture.

Mongolian Altai panoramas - from Ölgii's heights, the Altai peaks dominate the horizon at over 4,000 meters. These mountains with permanent glaciers constitute one of Mongolia's most striking landscapes. From the city, spot the snow-capped massifs of Tsambagarav and Altai Tavan Bogd.

activities.text: Exploration of Ölgii's local market - the city bazaar is the best place to grasp daily life in this Kazakh community. Embroidered fabrics, felt blankets, worked leather objects: local craftsmanship is particularly rich. It's also an opportunity to stock up for the following days before reaching more isolated areas.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Mongolia Tour: Altai, Gobi, and Central Steppe

visits.text: Ongiin Khiid Monastery - founded in 1660 on both banks of the Ongi River, this complex at its peak housed over 1,000 monks across some thirty temples and four Buddhist universities. It was one of the largest religious centers in southern Mongolia. In 1939, under Khorloogiin Choibalsan's direction, the Communist army razed everything: 200 monks were executed, survivors imprisoned or deported. The river itself was diverted to supply mines. In 1990, three monks who had begun their education here sixty years earlier returned to the ruins and began rebuilding, stone by stone. Today one temple has been restored and two small museums display rescued religious objects, carved stones and original architectural elements.

Ruins of the north site (Barlim Temple) - on the other side of the river, the foundations and partially standing walls of Barlim Temple, the largest of all, give an idea of the scale of what existed. One of its temples was the largest in all Mongolia. Walk through the ruins along the water's edge.

activities.text: Hike in the surrounding hills - the site is surrounded by arid steppe hills that can be climbed for a view over the entire monastery and the Ongi River valley. An hour's walk is enough to reach a good viewpoint.
Swimming in the Ongi River - after a week of desert and off-road driving, the river winding at the monastery's foot offers a chance for a refreshing stop. The water is clear and shallow.

days_plan.itin_title: Mongolia Road Trip: Gobi Desert, Orkhon Valley & Lake Khövsgöl
Grand Mongolia Tour: Altai, Gobi, and Central Steppe

visits.text: Ulaan Tsutgalan Falls - the Ulaan Tsutgalan River ('the red confluence') plunges into the Orkhon over cliffs of black basalt in hexagonal columns, creating a waterfall about 20 meters high and 10 meters wide. These volcanic columns formed over 20,000 years ago during eruptions that covered the valley with lava. The contrast between white foam, black basalt and surrounding green steppe is striking. Flow varies greatly by season: very strong from June to August, the falls can be nearly dry in spring.

Basalt canyon downstream from the falls - descend the trail along the gorge downstream. The walls in volcanic organ pipes - those regular hexagonal columns reminiscent of the Giant's Causeway in Ireland - plunge 20 to 30 meters. Wild peonies grow at the cliff base in July.

Orkhon Valley landscape - from the plateau overlooking the river, the view embraces the valley's green grasslands, herds of yaks and horses, and white gers scattered across the folds of the terrain. This tableau has remained unchanged for centuries. The Huns, Uyghurs, Turks, then the Mongol Empire all chose this valley as their power base.

activities.text: Descent to the foot of the waterfall - a trail of about 1 km descends from the plateau to the basin at the foot of the falls. The descent is moderate but slippery after rain. From below, the view of the waterfall and basalt columns is far more impressive than from above.
Settling into the Orkhon Valley - arrival at ger camp by the river. The valley has been a UNESCO heritage site since 2004. Evening by the Orkhon, Mongolia's longest river (1,124 km).

Horseback riding in the valley - local operators offer 2 to 4-hour horseback rides through the grasslands and along the Orkhon River. The Mongolian horse is small and sturdy, accustomed to the terrain. It's one of the most authentic ways to explore the valley, as herders have done for centuries.

Immersion with a nomadic family - families settled in the valley often welcome travelers for salted milk tea, dried cheese (aaruul) and fermented milk vodka (arkhi). A chance to observe how a ger is assembled, how mares are milked to make koumiss, how a steppe herder's day unfolds.

days_plan.itin_title: Mongolia Road Trip: Gobi Desert, Orkhon Valley & Lake Khövsgöl
Mongolia Road Trip: Gobi Desert, Orkhon Valley & Lake Khövsgöl
Grand Mongolia Tour: Altai, Gobi, and Central Steppe
Grand Mongolia Tour: Altai, Gobi, and Central Steppe

places.name: Terkhiin Tsagaan Nuur

visits.text: Tsetserleg and Zayan Gegeen Monastery - capital of Arkhangai Province, Tsetserleg is often considered the most pleasant and greenest of Mongolia's provincial towns. Zayan Gegeen Monastery, founded in the mid-17th century, was converted into an ethnographic and religious museum in 1997. It displays tangkas, religious objects, traditional musical instruments and elements of nomadic life. A few hours' stop en route westward.

Lake Terkhiin Tsagaan Nuur (the White Lake) - at 2,060 meters altitude in the Khangai Mountains, this lake was born about 8,000 years ago from the eruption of Khorgo volcano, whose lava flow dammed the Suman River. It stretches 16 km long and up to 10 km wide (61 km²), with an average depth of 20 meters. Its waters are so pure you can drink them directly. Listed as a Ramsar site in 1998. The shores are surrounded by grasslands, basalt formations and the craters of other extinct volcanoes.

Khorgo Volcano - this extinct volcano peaks at 2,240 meters east of the lake. Its last eruption was about 8,000 years ago, making it Mongolia's most recently active volcano. The climb to the crater rim, about 200 meters in diameter and 70 to 80 meters deep, offers a 360° view over White Lake and the Arkhangai steppe. Protected since 1965.

Lava fields and 'basalt gers' - around Khorgo, vast solidified lava flows form a lunar landscape locals call 'the basalt gers': solidified lava bubbles resembling domes. Lava tubes run beneath the surface of some areas.

activities.text: Tsetserleg local market en route - ideal supply point before the more isolated stages northward. Dried fruits, preserves, dried cheeses (aaruul), leather goods. Also a chance to see Tsetserleg, a town nestled in a mountain valley.
Settling in by White Lake - arrival late afternoon. Stroll along the lake's south shore, in peace. The evening light on the lake and surrounding wooded hills is particularly beautiful.

Climbing Khorgo volcano - from the camp by the lake, hike on foot to the crater rim. The trail crosses basalt fields and larch groves. The climb takes 1 to 2 hours depending on pace. View of the lake from the crater edge.

Kayak or canoe outing on White Lake - local operators offer watercraft to explore the lake surface. The waters are clear to the bottom. In summer, families of mallards and whooper swans frequent the shores.

days_plan.itin_title: Mongolia Road Trip: Gobi Desert, Orkhon Valley & Lake Khövsgöl
Mongolia Road Trip: Gobi Desert, Orkhon Valley & Lake Khövsgöl
Grand Mongolia Tour: Altai, Gobi, and Central Steppe
Grand Mongolia Tour: Altai, Gobi, and Central Steppe

map_keywords: Terkhiin Tsagaan Nuur White Lake Mongolia
Terkhiin Tsagaan Nuur lava field Mongolia

visits.text: Tsagaan Suvarga (the White Stupa) - this limestone formation 60 meters high and 400 meters long rises from the Gobi plain like the ruins of a ghost city. Millions of years ago, Dundgovi Province lay at the bottom of an inland sea; the sedimentary layers exposed by erosion tell, in their striped colors - cream white, orange, violet, ochre red - the different geological epochs. Stand on the east side to see the entire cliff face: from there it resembles a Buddhist stupa, which earned it its name. At sunset, the colors shift to deep red - the best time for photography.
Red Stupa (Ulaan Suvarga) - about 20 km southwest of the White Stupa, a similar formation with more pronounced red tones. Less visited, it's worth the detour if you have time late in the day.

activities.text: Hike along the White Stupa ridge - from the parking area on top of the plateau, descend along the cliff edge to view the colors of the wall from below. Allow an hour round trip. Beware the edge: the rock is friable and erosion has carved overhangs.

Camelback ride at the foot of the cliffs - local operators offer rides on Bactrian camels (two-humped) in the semi-desert around the site. A way to see the landscape at human height and meet the nomadic camel-herding families of the region.

days_plan.itin_title: Mongolia Road Trip: Gobi Desert, Orkhon Valley & Lake Khövsgöl
Grand Mongolia Tour: Altai, Gobi, and Central Steppe

visits.text: Zaya Gegeen Monastery-Museum - perched on Tsetserleg's heights, this monastery was founded by Zaya Pandita Luvsanperenlei (1642-1715), the first great Khalkh religious figure, who made it the heart of a Buddhist principality. Converted into an ethnographic and religious museum, it presents a collection of objects from nomadic life, costumes, and traditional musical instruments. The hilltop view over the city and forested mountains is worth the visit.

Tsetserleg city center - its name means 'garden' in Mongolian, and the city truly deserves this reputation: capital of Arkhangai province nestled at 1,700 meters altitude, it's considered Mongolia's greenest and most pleasant provincial city. Stroll the streets, spot Soviet architecture mixed with contemporary buildings, and do some shopping at the local market.

activities.text: Descent Terkhiin Tsagaan Nuur → Tsetserleg - about 180 km southeast (3 to 3.5 hours). The road crosses the Khangai foothills, alternating larch forests, passes, and verdant valleys. The landscape gradually softens approaching Tsetserleg, the country's greenest provincial capital.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Mongolia Tour: Altai, Gobi, and Central Steppe

visits.text: Gandantegchinlen Monastery - built in 1809 as Shar Sum ('the yellow monastery') by the 5th Javzandamba, it took its current name in 1838 when relocated to Dalkha Hill. Inside, the statue of Megjid-Janraiseg towers over 26 meters high and weighs more than 20 tons of copper - remade in 1996 with gold donations from Nepal and Japan after the Soviets melted down the original in 1937. Hundreds of monks still live here today. Arrive early in the morning to witness the daily Buddhist ceremonies - worshippers come in droves, the monastery is fully alive.

Sükhbaatar Square - in the heart of the capital, this grand plaza bears the name of revolutionary hero Damdin Sükhbaatar, who proclaimed Mongolia's independence here in 1921. The Government Palace closes off the far end of the square, with its large equestrian statue of Genghis Khan. This is where the political and symbolic heart of the nation beats.

National Museum of Mongolia - the museum traces the country's history from Bronze Age nomads through the Soviet era. Traditional costumes, Mongol Empire weaponry, shamanic instruments and a reconstructed ger are on display. Allow about 1.5 hours for a thorough visit.

Zaisan Memorial - perched on a hill south of the city, this memorial built for the 50th anniversary of the Revolution honors Soviet-Mongolian soldiers who died during World War II. The circular reinforced concrete panel illustrates Russian-Mongolian friendship through four scenes: the 1921 independence, the 1939 Battle of Khalkhin Gol, victory over Nazi Germany, and space exploration. Climb the 300 steps from the parking area - the panoramic view over Ulaanbaatar and the Tuul River is the best in the capital.

Return to Ulaanbaatar - about 340 km from Iven Valley, passing through Darkhan, largely on paved road. The track out from the monastery (40 km) is the only difficult section; after Darkhan the road is fast. Arrival late afternoon in the capital. End of circuit.

activities.text: Stroll through downtown streets between Sükhbaatar Square and the shopping districts - a chance to see Soviet-era apartment blocks, urban gers on the outskirts, and glass towers in the center all coexisting.
Sunset from Zaisan Hill - the best time to enjoy the view, with raking light across the plain and the Bogd Khan mountains in the background.

Last shopping in Ulaanbaatar - downtown (State Department Store, Narantuul market) is the place to find quality souvenirs: Mongolian cashmere, felt work, silver jewelry, thangka reproductions. Mongolian cashmere is reputed among the world's finest.
Farewell dinner in a capital restaurant - return to the city after 19 days of steppe and off-road tracks. Ulaanbaatar has quality restaurants; a chance to dine somewhere comfortable before departure.

days_plan.itin_title: Mongolia Road Trip: Gobi Desert, Orkhon Valley & Lake Khövsgöl
Mongolia Road Trip: Gobi Desert, Orkhon Valley & Lake Khövsgöl
Grand Mongolia Tour: Altai, Gobi, and Central Steppe
Grand Mongolia Tour: Altai, Gobi, and Central Steppe

visits.text: Uliastai, capital of Zavkhan - one of Mongolia's oldest cities, founded in 1733 as a Manchu garrison by the Qing dynasty. At its peak, the fortress housed 3,500 soldiers. Today, traces of this era remain on the Bogd River banks: ruins of the former governor's fortress are visible a few minutes' walk from the center. Uliastai is also an essential supply point before continuing east.

Zavkhan People's Museum - the provincial museum presents the life and customs of Zavkhan nomads: reconstructed yurts, utensils, festival costumes, and traditional musical instruments. A useful stop for understanding this region that tourism still rarely visits.

activities.text: Crossing Zavkhan - the circuit's wildest stage. The sandy or rocky track between Hyargas Nuur and Uliastai (about 300 km, 6 to 7 hours) crosses nearly deserted semi-arid steppes. There's no signage: your driver must know the cross-country tracks. A robust vehicle like a Land Cruiser is essential. Enjoy a few stops along the way to observe the landscape and, if the season permits, Mongolian gazelles on the plains.
